narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 grade decile, your LSAT score, and your family income bracket. Most influential was your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades, which is notably lower than the near-top deciles typical for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance through law school. Close behind was your LSAT score of 25, well below the average of about 37 for bar passers, which the model sees as a key early indicator of exam readiness. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income aligned well with the majority of successful students—around 45% of bar passers came from similar backgrounds—helping to nudge the prediction slightly toward success. Compared to admitted students, who often enter with undergraduate GPAs around 3.24 and stronger early deciles like 6 or higher, your profile in these areas stood out as riskier, even with your full-time study plans matching the norm for passers. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and Year 1 decile of 2 also factored in, they played smaller roles here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—it's the model highlighting areas where past students with similar profiles faced hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your upper-level grades if possible, or exploring programs with bridge courses or bar prep support tailored for determined students like you. Many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements, and admissions teams value persistence. You might also seek feedback from advisors on building a stronger narrative around your unique path. Keep pushing—your story is still unfolding.
